summary: Patrick McKenzie reflects on the psychological difficulty of adjusting to self-employment after a lifetime of externally imposed schedules. Despite having complete freedom over his time, he finds the lack of structure disorienting and struggles with the feeling that he isn't being productive enough. Paradoxically, data shows he's actually getting more done than ever—running more A/B tests and increasing sales. He attributes the disconnect to decades of measuring productivity by hours worked rather than output. He explores hacks like getting an office for mental separation and using a gamification app to maintain discipline.
key_points: Complete schedule freedom is psychologically destabilizing after a lifetime of externally imposed structure, even when it's objectively productive
Self-employed people can feel unproductive despite data showing increased output, because they've been conditioned to equate productivity with time-at-desk
Decoupling customer happiness from personal availability is essential for running a global software business, but removes the urgency that drives daily work
Social validation of employment is deeply embedded—from waitresses questioning your schedule to police officers questioning your immigration status
Gamification and environmental hacks (offices, apps) can substitute for the discipline that schedules previously provided
money_quotes: They exist like the Greek gods: you didn't ask for them but they are there, there is no negotiating with them, and prolonged association means you are likely to get your dignity violated by a bovine.
Gainfully employed young men should be working at 2 PM on a Wednesday. I was having a late lunch and reading a novel at the coffee shop.
That's the thing about schedules: once you have one everyone else needs to, too, preferably as close to yours as possible.

I spend lots of effort decoupling their happiness from my personal availability. This does wonderful things for site uptime but it also means, perhaps regrettably, that there is generally no compulsion to work today.

It turns out that I am smoking something: I ran a shell script to compare my productivity prior and post quitting. I thought it would show me spinning my wheels. Turns out I am getting more done than ever.
I think after a couple of decades of living by the clock I have become habituated to measuring my productivity that way. Insane and irrational, I know.
Sounds funny until it happens from a police officer who does not quite understand immigration statuses.
key_insight: The hardest part of self-employment isn't doing the work—it's the psychological disorientation of losing externally imposed structure, which can make you feel unproductive even when data proves you're accomplishing more than ever.
